Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd drift off and do other things instead. I usually ended up cramming in study sessions whenever I remembered or felt a sudden wave of guilt. With 15 online courses, it was overwhelming to even figure out where to start, so I'd often just pick whatever felt easiest in the moment.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be up late trying to catch up before a deadline. Regular schedules never really stuck for me—life just felt too unpredictable, and I'd get anxious about everything I wasn't doing, which paradoxically made me avoid studying more.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due, and that would jolt me into action. Occasionally I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but I rarely stayed long unless I absolutely had to.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check it daily, but that motivation would fade within a week. It always felt like a chore rather than something integrated into my routine.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see concepts being explained. I watched quite a bit of video content when I did engage. The visual diagrams and demonstrations helped things click in ways that reading text just didn't accomplish for me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time—I only posted three times the whole course. They felt awkward and time-consuming, and my anxiety made me overthink every comment I might post. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because there was just too much material across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to assignments or exams. If something would directly help me pass, I'd engage with it. Abstract or supplementary stuff that seemed optional? I'd skip it because my mental bandwidth was already maxed out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're juggling 15 courses and feeling anxious about everything, optional just means 'skip this.' Required activities got done because they had to, but even then I'd often do the bare minimum to get through.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from my tablet offline, especially when I was too anxious to be online and see all the notifications about things I was behind on. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to understand concepts better.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened while I was reviewing downloaded materials or rewatching videos I'd saved. I'd spend hours going through my notes and screenshots of diagrams, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best studying often happened disconnected from the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, maybe take some screenshots of key visuals, and then review those repeatedly offline. It was less overwhelming than navigating the platform constantly, and I could study without triggering my anxiety about seeing how much I still had to do.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked when I realized how behind I was across multiple courses, and instead of catching up, I kind of froze. There were weeks where I could barely bring myself to log in because seeing my incomplete tasks made me feel worse.
Q13: Absolutely. The sheer workload from 15 courses created this constant fog of exhaustion and confusion. When I was really stressed, I'd log into the LMS, feel overwhelmed by everything, and then close it without doing much. Fatigue made it hard to process information even when I tried.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos or readings and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxiety about other deadlines or life stuff, so even though I was technically 'engaging' with materials, nothing was sinking in.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they triggered my anxiety about being judged. I also avoided starting assignments until the last minute because the thought of doing them felt paralyzing. I knew it was important, but knowing and doing are different things when you're anxious.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and avoid. But when something was due soon, I'd suddenly become productive—though that last-minute rush was stressful and probably hurt the quality of my learning.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost and made me feel like maybe I could handle this. But that feeling faded quickly. Honestly, I scored well on assignments when I submitted them, so the feedback confirmed I could do it—I just struggled with consistency.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it harder for someone like me. Without strict weekly requirements, I'd let things slide. More structure might have forced me to stay on track instead of drifting until deadlines hit.
Q19: Having so much content available at once was overwhelming. I wished things were released more gradually so I couldn't see the mountain of work ahead. The visual resources were helpful though—anything with diagrams or videos worked well for how I learn.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching bits of videos, but not actually completing anything because the anxiety makes it hard to commit to submitting work. You're active but not productive.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed, and my assignment scores were really high when I actually submitted. A lot of my real studying happened offline, so low LMS activity didn't mean I wasn't learning—just that I wasn't doing it on the platform.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually focused or just had something open while zoning out. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, and whether engagement was meaningful or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when your mind is present and you're genuinely trying to learn. For me, true engagement was rare this semester—mostly I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it took just to log in some days. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, or how the CBT sessions actually helped improve my mood by the end. My emotional journey through this course was completely invisible to the data.
